rationale: The chain shows PowerShell spawning cmd.exe (EV-001) which copies a binary to both 'C:\Program Files\windows_service.exe' and 'C:\program.exe' (a classic unquoted service path abuse staging location), creates and starts a new service (A0, EV-002), and the service binary runs as SYSTEM via services.exe (EV-003). The cleanup sequence (EV-004) — stopping and deleting the service, removing both binaries and a 'C:\Time.log' file — is consistent with a controlled test or red-team exercise (e.g., a persistence/privilege-escalation proof-of-concept), but is equally consistent with an attacker covering tracks after achieving SYSTEM execution. The obfuscated source path '[x]\[x]\[id]\bin\' and the deliberate artifact deletion prevent full attribution. Key missing evidence: no file-creation or network events to assess the binary's origin or behavior; no parent context above the PowerShell process; the binary hashes are not corroborated against known-good or known-bad repositories. The activity warrants investigation to determine whether this was an authorized test or unauthorized persistence.
